words.shortDefinition: Move or combine a sequence of commits onto a new base commit hash.
words.simpleDefinition: Replaying local commits on top of another branch's latest state — rewriting history so specific changes appear as if they were written after the latest updates. Results in a cleaner, linear project history without merge commits.
words.longDefinition: Rebase rewrites project history by creating brand new commits for each snapshot in the original branch, effectively changing their parentage. It is primarily used to integrate changes from a target branch into a feature branch while maintaining a single line of development. This avoids the clutter of 'diamond' shapes in the commit graph, though it requires force-pushing if the branch was already shared.
words.connector: Think of rebase like moving your bookmark to the end of a revised chapter — your notes now follow the very latest text.
words.techContext: I performed a rebase onto the main branch to ensure my feature branch included all the latest changes before I submitted my pull request.
words.exampleSentence: I performed an interactive rebase to clean up my commit history before submitting the final pull request for review.

codeExample.code:
git checkout feature
git rebase main

words.shortDefinition: Apply changes from specific existing commits onto the current active branch.
words.simpleDefinition: Selecting a specific commit from one branch and applying it to another — without merging entire branch histories. Ideal for pulling critical bug fixes into stable releases while leaving experimental features behind in development.
words.longDefinition: Cherry-pick enables surgical integration of changes by duplicating a specific commit's diff onto your current HEAD. Unlike a merge, which joins two branch histories, cherry-picking creates a brand new commit with the same content but a different hash. This is essential for backporting fixes to older versions or extracting specific improvements from a messy experimental branch.
words.connector: Think of cherry-pick like selecting one song from an album to add to your playlist — you get the track without the whole record.
words.techContext: We decided to cherry-pick the security patch from the 'v2.0-dev' branch into the 'production' branch.
words.exampleSentence: We decided to cherry-pick the security patch from the development branch into the production release to avoid deploying unfinished features.
words.etymology: Metaphor for selecting only the best or most desirable items from a group.

codeExample.code: git cherry-pick <commit-hash>

words.shortDefinition: Condense multiple sequential commits into a single, unified historical snapshot.
words.simpleDefinition: Combining multiple incremental commits into one polished entry — turning a messy series of 'fix typo' or 'work in progress' notes into a single meaningful record. This keeps the main branch history clean and highly readable.
words.longDefinition: Squashing is a technique used during an interactive rebase or merge to simplify the commit graph. It takes the diffs of several commits and rolls them into a single new commit with a unified message. This is standard practice in professional workflows where developers commit frequently during local development but want a clean, single-purpose entry for the final shared history.
words.connector: Think of squash like editing a rough draft into a final essay — all your points remain, but the messy revisions disappear.
words.techContext: Our team policy is to squash all commits in a pull request before merging to keep the main branch history concise.
words.exampleSentence: The team requires developers to squash their commits before merging to ensure each pull request appears as one logical unit.
words.etymology: from Old French esquasser ('to crush, break').

codeExample.code:
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# Change 'pick' to 'squash' for the second and third commits

words.shortDefinition: Temporarily store uncommitted local changes to clear the working directory.
words.simpleDefinition: Shelving uncommitted work to create a clean working tree — allowing immediate context switching between branches without losing progress. These saved changes can be reapplied later to any branch when you are ready to resume.
words.longDefinition: The stash acts as a local stack for dirty working directory states. When you run the command, Git saves your modified tracked files and staged changes into a temporary storage area and reverts your workspace to match the HEAD commit. This is invaluable for handling urgent bug fixes mid-task, as it avoids the need for 'checkpoint' commits that clutter the project history.
words.connector: Think of stash like a 'pause' button for your workspace — it freezes your current progress so you can handle something else.
words.techContext: I stashed my half-finished changes so I could quickly switch to the hotfix branch and resolve the production bug.
words.exampleSentence: I had to stash my UI changes so I could switch to the hotfix branch and resolve a critical production error.
words.etymology: Origin unknown, possibly a blend of 'stow' and 'cache'.

codeExample.code:
git stash save "Work on login form"
git stash pop

words.shortDefinition: A state where Git cannot automatically reconcile diverging changes between two branches.
words.simpleDefinition: Technical collision occurring when two branches modify the same code block in incompatible ways — requiring manual human intervention. Git inserts conflict markers to highlight the differences, forcing the developer to choose or combine the final logic.
words.longDefinition: Merge conflicts typically occur when two developers modify overlapping lines in a file or when one person deletes a file that another is currently editing. Git halts the merge process and marks the affected areas with arrows and equal signs. The developer must then manually edit the files, remove the markers, and stage the resolved version to complete the integration process.
words.connector: Think of a merge conflict like two people writing on the same line of a shared notebook — someone must decide which text stays.
words.techContext: I spent an hour resolving a merge conflict in the configuration file after two feature branches were merged simultaneously.
words.exampleSentence: I encountered a merge conflict in the database schema file after rebasing my branch onto the latest main updates.

codeExample.code:
<<<<<<< HEAD
const theme = 'dark';
=======
const theme = 'light';
>>>>>>> feature-branch

words.shortDefinition: An immutable snapshot of staged changes saved to the repository history.
words.simpleDefinition: Permanent record of your project at a specific moment — captured with a descriptive message and unique SHA-1 hash. It acts as the fundamental building block of Git history, allowing you to track progress or revert to any previous state.
words.longDefinition: A commit represents a single point in the project's timeline, containing a snapshot of the staged files, metadata about the author, and a pointer to the previous commit. By chaining these snapshots together, Git creates a complete, navigable history of the entire codebase. Every commit is identified by a unique cryptographic hash, ensuring data integrity and making the history virtually impossible to alter without detection.
words.connector: Think of a commit like saving your progress in a video game — it's a permanent checkpoint you can always return to.
words.techContext: I made a commit with a descriptive message so my teammates would understand why I changed the API endpoint.
words.exampleSentence: You should strive to make small, atomic commits that focus on solving a single problem or implementing one specific feature.

codeExample.code:
git add config.js
git commit -m "Update API timeout settings"

words.shortDefinition: Central storage location for project files, history, and version control metadata.
words.simpleDefinition: Comprehensive storage unit containing all project files alongside their complete historical record — including every commit, branch, and tag. A repository can reside locally on your workstation or remotely on a central server to facilitate team collaboration.
words.longDefinition: Commonly referred to as a 'repo', it is the core container for a project managed by Git. Unlike traditional backup systems, a Git repository stores compressed snapshots of every file version, allowing for efficient branching and merging. It consists of the working directory, the staging area, and the '.git' directory, which holds the actual object database and configuration that defines the project's evolution.
words.connector: Think of a repository like a library's master archive — it contains every book and every revision ever made to them.
words.techContext: I cloned the repository from GitHub to my local machine so I could start working on the new feature.
words.exampleSentence: I initialized a new Git repository in the project folder to start tracking my changes from the very beginning.
words.etymology: from Latin repositorium ('a place for storing').

codeExample.code:
git init
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

words.shortDefinition: Prepare specific file modifications for inclusion in the next commit snapshot.
words.simpleDefinition: Marking selected changes for the upcoming commit — creating a middle-ground 'index' between your workspace and the permanent history. This allows you to group related modifications together while leaving unrelated experiments or debug logs out of the official record.
words.longDefinition: Staging is a unique feature of Git that provides a buffer known as the 'index'. Instead of committing every change in your working directory at once, you use the 'git add' command to selectively pick which files or specific hunks of code are ready. This granular control is essential for creating clean, logical commits that only contain changes relevant to a single task or fix.
words.connector: Think of staging like a photographer's 'selects' folder — you choose only the best shots before publishing them to the album.
words.techContext: I only staged the bug fix, leaving my experimental changes unstaged so they wouldn't be included in the commit.
words.exampleSentence: After reviewing my changes, I staged only the modified CSS files while keeping the experimental JavaScript changes unstaged.
words.etymology: from Latin stagium ('a place for standing').

codeExample.code:
git add index.html
git add -p style.css # Stage specific hunks

words.shortDefinition: Display line-by-line differences between files, commits, branches, or the index.
words.simpleDefinition: Powerful command revealing precise modifications between file versions — identifying exactly what was added, removed, or changed. It is essential for auditing work before staging and for understanding how the codebase has evolved over time across different branches.
words.longDefinition: Git diff generates a patch-style output that highlights changes using '+' for additions and '-' for deletions. It can compare your current working directory against the staging area, or compare two specific commit hashes to see what changed between them. Developers rely on this tool during code reviews and debugging to pinpoint the exact lines of code that introduced a new behavior or bug.
words.connector: Think of git diff like a 'track changes' view in a document — it shows exactly what was deleted and what was added.
words.techContext: I used git diff to review my changes one last time before staging them for the commit.
words.exampleSentence: I used git diff to verify that my refactoring didn't accidentally delete any critical validation logic in the controller.

codeExample.code:
git diff # Working directory vs Staging
git diff --staged # Staged vs HEAD

words.shortDefinition: Text file specifying patterns of files and directories to exclude from tracking.
words.simpleDefinition: Configuration file defining which local assets Git should intentionally ignore — typically excluding heavy build artifacts, sensitive API keys, or temporary log files from the repository history. This ensures that only essential source code is shared and archived.
words.longDefinition: The .gitignore file uses glob patterns to filter out files that don't belong in version control. By excluding environment variables, IDE settings, and compiled binaries, you keep the repository lightweight and prevent security leaks. This file is itself version-controlled, ensuring that all team members maintain a consistent environment and avoid accidentally committing 'noise' that could clutter the shared history or break builds.
words.connector: Think of .gitignore like a filter on a camera — it blocks out the glare so only the important image gets captured.
words.techContext: I added '*.log' to the .gitignore file so my local debug logs wouldn't get pushed to the repository.
words.exampleSentence: I added the 'dist' and 'build' directories to the .gitignore file to prevent compiled assets from bloating the repository.

codeExample.code:
node_modules/
.env
*.log
.DS_Store

words.shortDefinition: Git state where the HEAD pointer points to a specific commit hash.
words.simpleDefinition: Temporary state where the active pointer identifies a specific commit rather than a named branch — meaning any new work is not associated with a timeline. This is useful for inspecting older versions, but risky for making permanent changes.
words.longDefinition: Normally, HEAD points to a named branch like 'main', which automatically moves forward when you commit. In a detached state, HEAD points directly to a commit SHA-1. While you can still make commits, they have no branch reference and will become 'orphaned' once you switch away. To save work done in this state, you must create a new branch to anchor those commits into the history.
words.connector: Think of detached HEAD like stepping off a train onto the tracks — you can see where you are, but you're no longer moving.
words.techContext: I ended up in a detached HEAD state because I checked out an old commit to see when a specific bug was introduced.
words.exampleSentence: I entered a detached HEAD state to test a specific bug fix from last month without affecting my current feature development.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'head' (pointer) that has been separated from its 'body' (branch).

codeExample.code:
git checkout 5f6a1c3
# You are now in 'detached HEAD' state.

words.shortDefinition: Direct pointer update that occurs when merging a direct descendant branch.
words.simpleDefinition: Optimized merge occurring when the current branch has not diverged from the target — allowing Git to simply slide the pointer forward to the latest commit. This avoids creating a 'merge commit' and results in a perfectly clean, linear timeline.
words.longDefinition: A fast-forward merge is possible only when there is a direct linear path from the current branch tip to the target commit. Because there are no competing changes to reconcile, Git doesn't need to perform a three-way merge. Instead, it just updates the branch reference. While this keeps history simple, some teams prefer using '--no-ff' to force a merge commit, which preserves the context of the feature integration.
words.connector: Think of a fast-forward merge like fast-forwarding a video tape — you're just skipping ahead to the latest part of the same story.
words.techContext: Since no one else had committed to main, my merge was a simple fast-forward that didn't require any conflict resolution.
words.exampleSentence: Since I was the only one working on the repo, my pull request resulted in a clean fast-forward merge into the main branch.

codeExample.code:
git checkout main
git merge feature-branch --ff-only

words.shortDefinition: Download objects and refs from another repository without updating local branch heads.
words.simpleDefinition: Retrieving the latest commits and metadata from a remote server into your local database — without modifying your active working files. It allows you to safely inspect what others have contributed before deciding to integrate those changes into your own branches.
words.longDefinition: Fetch is the 'safe' way to sync with a remote. It updates the remote-tracking branches, such as 'origin/main', but leaves your local 'main' branch untouched. This gives developers the opportunity to use 'git log' or 'git diff' to review incoming work. In contrast, 'git pull' is a shortcut that performs a fetch followed immediately by a merge, which can lead to unexpected conflicts if not careful.
words.connector: Think of fetch like downloading a weather report — you know what's coming, but you haven't stepped outside into the rain yet.
words.techContext: I ran 'git fetch' to see if there were any new commits on the server before I started my own work.
words.exampleSentence: I always run 'git fetch' first to see how many commits I'm behind before I decide to pull and update my local branch.
words.etymology: from Old English feccan ('to bring back').

codeExample.code:
git fetch origin
git log HEAD..origin/main

words.shortDefinition: Formal proposal to merge code changes from one branch into another branch.
words.simpleDefinition: Request for project maintainers to review and integrate your contributions — serving as a dedicated space for code discussion, automated testing, and peer feedback. This workflow ensures that new code meets quality standards before it enters the primary production history.
words.longDefinition: Commonly abbreviated as PR, this is a cornerstone of collaborative development on platforms like GitHub or Bitbucket. It allows a developer to notify team members that a feature is complete and ready for review. During the PR process, reviewers can comment on specific lines of code, request changes, and trigger CI/CD pipelines to ensure the new code doesn't break existing functionality or introduce security vulnerabilities.
words.connector: Think of a pull request like submitting a draft to an editor — you've written the story, but it needs a second look.
words.techContext: I opened a pull request for the new feature and tagged the senior developer for a code review.
words.exampleSentence: I submitted a pull request for the login fix and assigned the lead engineer to review my changes before the weekend.

codeExample.code:
# Usually performed via a web interface after:
git push origin feature-branch

words.shortDefinition: Independent server-side copy of a repository that remains linked to the original.
words.simpleDefinition: Personal copy of an existing repository hosted on your own account — allowing for complete creative freedom without impacting the original source. It is the standard starting point for open-source contributions, enabling developers to build features and then submit them back.
words.longDefinition: Unlike a local clone, a fork exists on the hosting service (like GitHub) and maintains a relationship with the 'upstream' repository. This setup allows you to push changes to your own version and later create pull requests to the original project. It is essential for community-driven development, as it allows anyone to experiment with a codebase without needing direct write access to the main production repository.
words.connector: Think of a fork like taking a branch from a tree to plant in your own garden — it grows independently now.
words.techContext: I forked the open-source library on GitHub so I could fix a bug and then submit a pull request back to the original author.
words.exampleSentence: I forked the popular web framework so I could add a custom plugin without waiting for the maintainers' permission.
words.etymology: Metaphor from a 'fork in the road' (splitting into two paths).

codeExample.code:
# Performed via GitHub UI "Fork" button
git clone https://github.com/my-user/forked-repo.git

words.shortDefinition: Binary search tool used to find the specific commit that introduced a regression.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated debugging tool that identifies the exact commit where a bug first appeared — by systematically testing versions between a known 'good' and 'bad' state. This binary search method significantly speeds up the process of finding regressions in large projects.
words.longDefinition: Git bisect automates the hunt for bugs by splitting the commit history in half repeatedly. You start by marking the current broken state as 'bad' and a previous stable state as 'good'. Git then checks out the middle commit for you to test. By repeating this process, you can find the culprit in a history of 1,000 commits in just 10 steps, making it an incredibly efficient debugging technique.
words.connector: Think of git bisect like finding a broken bulb in a string of lights by checking the middle of each section.
words.techContext: We used git bisect to find the exact change that caused the performance regression in our image rendering module.
words.exampleSentence: We used git bisect to track down a subtle memory leak that had been introduced somewhere over the last three hundred commits.

codeExample.code:
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD
git bisect good v1.2.0
# Test the code...
git bisect good

words.shortDefinition: Create a new commit that applies inverse changes to undo a previous commit.
words.simpleDefinition: Generating a specialized snapshot that reverses the effects of an earlier change — without altering the existing historical timeline. This creates a transparent audit trail, making it the safest way to correct mistakes on shared branches without disrupting other teammates.
words.longDefinition: Unlike 'git reset', which removes commits from the history, 'git revert' simply adds a new one that contains the exact opposite changes of the target commit. This is crucial for collaborative environments because it doesn't require force-pushing or rewriting shared history. It preserves the full story of the project, including the mistake and the subsequent fix, which is vital for maintaining a clean and honest development record.
words.connector: Think of git revert like writing a check to cancel out a previous payment — the bank statement shows both transactions.
words.techContext: I had to run 'git revert' on the broken deployment commit to quickly restore the site to a working state.
words.exampleSentence: The production build failed, so I had to quickly run 'git revert' on the latest merge to restore the website.

codeExample.code:
git revert <commit-hash>
# This creates a new "Revert '...'" commit

words.shortDefinition: Primary source repository from which a fork or local copy was derived.
words.simpleDefinition: Authoritative parent repository where the original project development occurs — acting as the source for all official updates and security patches. Keeping an 'upstream' remote reference allows you to sync your personal fork with the latest progress made by the main maintainers.
words.longDefinition: In collaborative workflows, 'upstream' refers to the main project you are contributing to. When you fork a repo, your version is 'downstream'. To keep your fork current, you must periodically fetch changes from the upstream remote and merge them into your local branches. This ensures you are always working on top of the most recent code and helps prevent painful merge conflicts when you eventually submit your work.
words.connector: Think of upstream like a news agency — you get the latest reports from the source to update your local paper.
words.techContext: I added a remote called 'upstream' that points to the original repository so I can pull the latest security updates.
words.exampleSentence: I set the original framework repo as my upstream remote so I could pull in the latest version 2.0 features.
words.etymology: Metaphor from river navigation: moving toward the source.

codeExample.code:
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git
git fetch upstream

words.shortDefinition: Default alias for the remote server repository used during initial cloning.
words.simpleDefinition: Standard nickname Git automatically assigns to the primary remote server where your project is hosted. It serves as the default destination for your 'push' and 'pull' operations, representing the central hub where you and your teammates share and synchronize work.
words.longDefinition: When you clone a repo, Git assumes you will want to interact with that same server again, so it creates the 'origin' remote as a shortcut. While you can technically rename it or add multiple other remotes, 'origin' remains the industry-standard convention for your primary project home. It is where your local branches typically 'track' their remote counterparts to simplify the workflow of staying up to date.
words.connector: Think of origin like your home address — it's the specific place you go back to and send mail from.
words.techContext: I pushed my new branch to origin so that my team members could see it on GitHub.
words.exampleSentence: I pushed my feature branch to origin so the CI/CD pipeline could start running the automated test suite on GitHub.

codeExample.code:
git remote -v
# origin https://github.com/user/repo.git (fetch)
# origin https://github.com/user/repo.git (push)

words.shortDefinition: Custom scripts triggered automatically by specific Git lifecycle events and actions.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated event-driven scripts that run during key repository operations — such as committing, merging, or pushing. These tools allow teams to enforce code quality standards, run unit tests automatically, or prevent sensitive secrets from being accidentally uploaded to a public server.
words.longDefinition: Hooks live in the '.git/hooks' directory and can be written in any scripting language. Client-side hooks like 'pre-commit' are used to lint code before it is finalized, while server-side hooks like 'pre-receive' can reject pushes that don't meet specific project requirements. They are essential for maintaining a high standard of consistency across a distributed team without requiring manual oversight for every single commit.
words.connector: Think of git hooks like an automatic sprinkler system — it turns on the moment it detects a specific condition.
words.techContext: We set up a pre-push hook to ensure that the entire test suite passes before code is sent to the server.
words.exampleSentence: Our project uses a pre-commit hook to automatically format the code and check for syntax errors before allowing a save.

codeExample.code:
# Example file: .git/hooks/pre-commit
npm run lint
npm test

words.shortDefinition: Switch between different project versions by updating files in the working directory.
words.simpleDefinition: Navigating between various branches or historical commits to update your active project files. This command allows you to jump between different feature timelines instantly, ensuring your local workspace perfectly matches the state of the code at that specific point in time.
words.longDefinition: Checkout is one of Git's most versatile tools, used for switching your active branch, creating new ones, or even restoring specific files to an earlier state. When you checkout a branch, Git updates your local files and pointers to reflect that branch's history. In recent versions of Git, this command has been partially superseded by the more specific 'git switch' and 'git restore' commands to reduce confusion.
words.connector: Think of checkout like changing a TV channel — the screen instantly shows a completely different program from the same source.
words.techContext: I checked out the 'bugfix-123' branch to investigate the issue reported by the customer.
words.exampleSentence: I checked out the documentation branch to fix a typo before returning to my work on the core API.
words.etymology: from the concept of checking out a book from a library.

codeExample.code:
git checkout main
git checkout -b new-feature # Create and switch

words.shortDefinition: A movable pointer to a specific commit for parallel project development.
words.simpleDefinition: Independent timeline for code changes — allowing multiple developers to work on isolated features or bug fixes simultaneously without interfering with the main project. In Git, branches are incredibly lightweight and fast, making them the primary tool for organized collaboration.
words.longDefinition: Technically, a branch is just a simple text file that contains the 40-character SHA-1 hash of the commit it points to. This makes creating and destroying branches nearly instantaneous. By using branches, teams can maintain a stable 'main' version of their software while dozens of experimental features are developed in parallel, only merging them back when they are fully tested and ready for production.
words.connector: Think of a branch like a parallel universe — you can change everything there without affecting the world you came from.
words.techContext: We created a separate branch for the experimental dark mode UI so we could work on it without breaking the main application.
words.exampleSentence: We always create a new branch for every Jira ticket to keep our development history organized and easy to review.
words.etymology: from Latin branca ('paw, branch of a tree').

codeExample.code:
git branch feature-x
git checkout feature-x

words.shortDefinition: Display the chronological record of commits and historical changes within a repository.
words.simpleDefinition: Detailed historical audit trail of your project — showing who made changes, when they happened, and the rationale behind them. This command is indispensable for navigating the timeline of a codebase and understanding how different features were built or where bugs originated.
words.longDefinition: The git log command provides a comprehensive view of the project's commit history, including the author's name, email, timestamp, and commit message. It can be filtered by date, author, or even specific files to help you find exactly when a piece of code changed. Advanced flags allow you to visualize the branch structure as a text-based graph, making complex merge histories easier to digest.
words.connector: Think of git log like a bank statement — it shows every single transaction that has ever happened in your account.
words.techContext: I used git log to see who last modified the authentication service and why.
words.exampleSentence: I used git log with the '--grep' flag to find all the commits related to the recent security patch.

codeExample.code:
git log --oneline --graph --all
git log -n 5 # Show last 5 commits

words.shortDefinition: Move the current branch pointer to a specific earlier state or commit.
words.simpleDefinition: Undo command that shifts your current branch backward in history — allowing you to unstage files or completely discard unwanted work. It is a powerful tool for cleaning up local mistakes before they are shared, though it must be used with caution.
words.longDefinition: Git reset operates in three main modes: soft, mixed, and hard. A 'soft' reset keeps your changes in the staging area; 'mixed' keeps them in your directory but unstages them; and 'hard' completely deletes all uncommitted changes to match the target commit exactly. Because 'hard' reset can cause permanent data loss, it should only be used when you are certain you want to abandon your current work.
words.connector: Think of git reset like a time machine — you can travel back to a moment before you made a mistake.
words.techContext: I used a 'soft reset' to undo my last three commits while keeping the actual code changes in my workspace so I could squash them.
words.exampleSentence: I performed a hard reset to the origin/main branch to completely wipe away my failed experimental changes and start fresh.

codeExample.code:
git reset --hard HEAD~1 # Delete last commit
git reset --soft HEAD~1 # Un-commit last commit

words.shortDefinition: Local log recording every change made to the repository's branch tips.
words.simpleDefinition: Invisible safety net tracking every movement of your local repository pointers — enabling the recovery of 'lost' commits that no longer belong to any branch. It serves as a detailed ledger of your local actions, recorded even when you rewrite history.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'Reference Log', the reflog is a purely local mechanism that Git uses to record when the tips of branches are updated. This is particularly useful after a complex rebase or an accidental hard reset, as it allows you to find the SHA-1 hashes of commits that are no longer reachable through the standard commit graph. It is the ultimate tool for rescuing work from nearly any Git disaster.
words.connector: Think of reflog like a browser history for your Git pointers — you can find where you were even if you closed the tab.
words.techContext: I thought I had lost my work after a messy rebase, but I found the commit hash in the reflog and was able to restore the branch.
words.exampleSentence: I used the reflog to find the hash of the feature branch I accidentally deleted and restored it in seconds.

codeExample.code:
git reflog
# Output: 4e2a9c1 HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
# Recover: git checkout 4e2a9c1
